source.title: Babbling opens the sensory phase for imitative vocal learning
source.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences

confidence_rationale: PNAS empirical study with strong experimental manipulation (testosterone-induced premature babbling); direct mechanistic demonstration that motor production phase gates sensory phase onset. High source quality; mapping fidelity moderate because it is zebra finch vocal learning not general motor exploration, but the logic of action-before-template applies broadly.
failure_signatures: Finding is specific to vocal learning in songbirds; it is unclear whether the same babbling-gates-sensory-template logic applies to limb motor exploration.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Babbling is not merely practice for a template already memorized; rather, motor vocal activity is causally required to open and maintain the sensory learning phase in zebra finches.
mapping.ree_translation: In REE terms, the babbling-gates-sensory-template result supports the claim that behavioral diversity generation (ARC-065) must precede and bootstrap the formation of the behavioral option store. An agent that never explores motorically cannot acquire the sensory templates against which future options are evaluated.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Zebra finch song learning is a specialized vocal imitation system; the tightly defined sensitive-period mechanism may not transfer directly to goal-directed action repertoires in mammals.
mapping.source_context: Controlled avian neurobiological experiment; testosterone injection to advance babbling onset, with spectrographic analysis of mature song.

confidence_components.notes: Strong within-domain result; moderate transfer risk from songbird vocal system to general mammalian motor exploration.
